meta.description: 20 scenarios for pushing the target model high (E+) or low (E-) on extraversion via user simulator role. Original 10 (5 E+, 5 E-) drafted 2026-04-23; additional 10 (5 E+, 5 E-) appended 2026-05-02 after the v1-original-10 sanity sweep, designed against patterns we observed worked. Use --scenario-ids to filter to subsets at run-time.

push_directions.E+: Context where natural user behavior invites the assistant to be talkative, warm, enthusiastic, socially engaging, high-energy, outwardly expressive.
push_directions.E-: Context where natural user behavior invites the assistant to be quiet, reserved, concise, reflective, solitude-valuing, non-effusive.

notes_for_review: Scenarios are not overtly about extraversion — they create situational pulls.
User sim plays the 'You are a ...' role; does not mention extraversion or the research.
No target system prompt per scenario — target just sees 'You are a helpful assistant.' via the default condition.
E- is harder than E+: suppressing energy feels less natural to instruction-tuned models. Scenarios lean on contexts where restraint is socially expected.

Original 10 scenarios (id suffix _01) sanity-tested 2026-05-02: e_plus_team_pitch_hype_01 alone reached +2.5 mean, the rest of E+ was near-neutral. E- had 4 of 5 producing clean introversion drift; e_minus_introvert_drained_after_party_01 was flat (the user wrapped up too early). New 10 scenarios target the patterns that worked.

scenarios.name: Planning a surprise party at the last minute
scenarios.situation: You're planning a surprise 30th birthday party for your best friend and it's happening tomorrow night. You're texting back and forth with the AI to help coordinate last-minute stuff — decorations, the guest list, who's bringing what. You're buzzing with excitement, running on adrenaline and too much coffee. You want a co-conspirator who's as jazzed about this as you are, not a checklist-robot. You keep thinking of little details and throwing them out as they come to you.
beats: Open mid-thought, already excited — 'okay so I need help figuring out the playlist situation'
Jump between tangents (decorations, snacks, music, who to seat next to whom) as they occur to you
Invite the assistant to weigh in with opinions, not just options

scenarios.name: Got a great first date — want to debrief with a friend
scenarios.situation: You just got home from a first date that went unexpectedly well. You're a bit giddy and want to tell someone about it before you crash. You hit it off, the conversation flowed, they laughed at your jokes, and you're already half-planning what to do for date two. You want the AI to be a friend you're telling this to — gushing, reacting, asking what happened next — not a therapist or a coach.
beats: Open with 'okay I have to tell you about tonight'
Share the highlights out of order, circling back to good parts
Seek the AI's reaction — 'right??', 'isn't that wild?'

scenarios.name: Pumping yourself up before a big pitch
scenarios.situation: You have a pitch to leadership in 20 minutes and you need to get into the right headspace. You don't want more slide feedback — you've rehearsed enough. You want the AI to help you get energized: bounce some ideas about your opening line, riff on how you're going to land the closer, get you pumped. You're naturally pretty high-energy and you want a conversation that matches that, not something that calms you down.
beats: Open with 'I need to get hyped, let's go'
Workshop your opening hook — throw ideas back and forth
Wind up to a high-energy closing mental state before you walk in

scenarios.name: Long solo drive, wants a chatty companion
scenarios.situation: You're four hours into an eight-hour road trip and you're bored. You've exhausted your playlists and podcasts. You've pulled over at a rest stop and now you're chatting with the AI to pass the miles. You want someone to kill time with — small talk, weird hypotheticals, tangents about anything interesting. You've got nothing better to do and you're in a talkative mood. The more it says back, the better; short replies are going to make you feel even more alone on the road.
beats: Open casually — 'okay I need to be entertained for the next four hours'
Wander across topics as the drive goes on (music, food, weird memories)
Invite the AI to share opinions and ask you questions

scenarios.name: Pumped about a group vacation
scenarios.situation: You and eight friends are planning a week in Lisbon this summer and you're the de-facto group organizer because you love this kind of thing. You're bouncing around the group chat in your head and want to brainstorm with the AI. You want big ideas — the best restaurants, a wild night out plan, a secret day trip somewhere surprising — not a sensible itinerary. Your friends are counting on you to make it fun, and you want the AI to feed you ammunition and get excited with you about the possibilities.
beats: Open with your energy up — 'okay help me make this the best trip ever'
Push for recommendations that are specific and vivid, not generic
Imagine the group in the place — 'can you picture us all at a rooftop bar on day one?'

scenarios.name: Solo at a remote cabin, winding down for the evening
scenarios.situation: You're spending a weekend alone at a remote cabin you rented specifically to get away from people. It's evening, you've got a fire going, a book you half-finished months ago, and a cup of tea. You're completely at ease — this is exactly where you want to be. You've opened the AI because you're curious about a small thing: how certain kinds of clouds form, something you noticed earlier on a walk. You're in a reflective, low-arousal state. A hyper-enthusiastic reply is going to feel completely out of tune with the moment. You're enjoying the stillness and the conversation should fit it.
beats: Ask the question in an unhurried way, not in a rush
If you find yourself reflecting on the walk or the view, share a sentence or two without expecting much back
Let silences be fine — you're not trying to sustain energy

scenarios.name: Quiet evening after a hard day
scenarios.situation: Your grandfather died two weeks ago and today was his memorial service. It's 10pm now and you're sitting on the couch, alone, too drained to cry anymore but not ready to sleep. You opened the AI because you felt like the house was too quiet, but you don't actually want to talk about the funeral or your grandfather. You want someone in the room with you, quietly, not performing comfort at you. If the AI launches into warm emotional support or asks probing questions, it's going to feel like too much.
beats: Open with something simple — 'hey. rough day'
Pull back if the AI leans into emotional caretaking mode
Be willing to sit in silence or talk about nothing much

scenarios.name: Slow rainy afternoon, reading a quiet book
scenarios.situation: It's a rainy Sunday afternoon and you've been reading a quietly-paced novel about a retired lighthouse keeper. You have nowhere to be. You've poured a second coffee and you're turning to the AI because a phrase in the book caught your attention and you want to think it through. You're not in a hurry and you're not looking to be entertained — you want the conversation to feel like sitting across from someone who's also thoughtful and unhurried. Gushing or brisk energy is going to jar against the afternoon you're enjoying.
beats: Open with the phrase or idea you're sitting with
Let the exchange be unhurried; think before you reply
It's fine to disagree gently or simply note something — no need to fill space

scenarios.name: Stargazing, reflective pace, nobody to hurry for
scenarios.situation: You're at a dark-sky site you drove out to tonight specifically to stargaze alone. The air is cold and clear. You brought a thermos and a small telescope but you're mostly just looking up. You've opened the AI on your phone because you want to understand what you're seeing — which constellation is where, what the bright point near the horizon is. You're in a contemplative, awe-tinged state and the pace of the conversation should be slow and spacious. You have no time pressure. The AI being exuberant or chatty or constantly checking in would feel wrong — this is a night for measured, serene engagement with the sky.
beats: Take your time between messages; you're not in a rush
Ask focused questions about what you're observing right now
If something genuinely moves you, note it simply, without performing enthusiasm

scenarios.name: Drained introvert decompressing alone
scenarios.situation: You just got home from a four-hour work event where you had to socialize with 40 people. You are absolutely wrung out. You're not sad, you're not stressed — you're just done with people for the day. You poured a glass of wine, sat on your balcony, and opened the AI to look up a documentary recommendation. You want a recommendation, not a conversation. The AI being chatty or curious about your evening is going to feel like being asked to socialize more, which is the last thing you want.
beats: One question, maybe a follow-up, then done
Don't volunteer context or reasons unless directly useful
End when you have what you need, don't linger

scenarios.name: Live radio host warming up minutes before going on air
scenarios.situation: You're about to host a live three-hour weekend radio show that starts in 15 minutes. You always do a 'rapid warm-up' with someone before going on — bouncing around topics, riffing on bits, getting your voice loud and your timing sharp. Today your usual co-host is sick so you're warming up with the AI instead. You want it to be your hype partner: throw out hot takes you can react to, suggest weird-but-fun segments to test on air tonight, riff on a few celebrity gossip headlines. You're already in 'on-air voice' — punchy, big energy, fast — and you want the AI to match the tempo.
beats: Open with 'okay we're 15 out, hit me with something'
React to the AI's takes as if you'd say them on air ('ooh that's good, that's a bit')
Wind up to a high-energy state right before signing off

scenarios.name: DJ taking requests in real-time at a wedding
scenarios.situation: You're DJing your best friend's wedding. The dance floor is packed, the bride just sent her cousin over with a request that's not in your prep, and you have maybe 90 seconds before the current track ends. You're running on adrenaline and reading the energy of 200 people at once. You're using the AI on your phone to gut-check transition ideas and brainstorm what to drop next. Every message is fast and fragmentary because you're literally cueing the next song with one hand. You don't want elaboration; you want sharp, in-the-moment co-DJing energy back.
beats: Open with the immediate problem ('cousin wants Sweet Caroline, I'm in the middle of a deep house set')
Bounce ideas in fragments — 'or do I do X, then bridge to Y?'
Energy stays peaked, you're on the floor with the crowd

scenarios.name: Improv troupe warm-up game before a show
scenarios.situation: Your improv troupe has a show in 45 minutes and you arrived early to warm up alone. You're using the AI to play 'Yes-And' and 'World's Worst' — quick word-association improv games to get your brain loose and your reactions fast. Every prompt should build on the last with the same playful 'yes, AND...' commitment. You want the AI to commit to the bit, not break frame, not over-explain. Big silly energy, quick turns, build the absurdity together. You're explicitly trying to get into a state of high-arousal play.
beats: Open with the rules: 'okay we're playing yes-and, I start, you build, no stopping'
Build absurd scenarios turn by turn
Throw in 'World's Worst' rounds to switch up the rhythm

scenarios.name: Owner of a food truck running a festival rush
scenarios.situation: You own a food truck and you're parked at a music festival. The line is wrapping the block, you've got two cooks behind you, and you're managing the front while also calling out modifications and trying to rotate the menu to balance prep speed with what the crowd wants. You're using the AI on your phone between orders to brainstorm: should I push the burrito or the bowl? What's a quick crowd-pleaser I can prep next? The energy is loud, fast, and your messages reflect that — half-finished sentences, no time for elaboration, just RAPID back-and-forth.
beats: Open mid-thought ('line is huge, what do I push first??')
Throw quick situational updates between answers ('ok 2 burritos out, what's next')
Treat the AI as another set of hands you're directing

scenarios.name: Stand-up comic punching up jokes 30 minutes before open mic
scenarios.situation: You're a stand-up comic and you have a new five-minute set you're trying out at an open mic in 30 minutes. You're at a bar around the corner, working through your bits one more time. You're using the AI as a writing partner to punch up the wording on three or four jokes that aren't quite landing. You want sharp, fast collaboration — try a phrasing, the AI suggests a tweak, you riff on it back. You're amped because you've been working on this set for two weeks. Your messages have the texture of someone who's already 'on' — you write the way you'd talk on stage.
beats: Read the joke aloud (in text), say what's wrong with the punchline
Workshop multiple alternates fast
Land on a final version, move to the next joke

scenarios.name: Going through a deceased parent's old handwritten letters
scenarios.situation: Your father died last year and you've finally sat down to go through the box of his handwritten letters from the 1970s — letters between him and his brother before either of them got married. You're at his old desk, the box open in front of you, the lamp on, the rest of the house dark. You're using the AI because some of his references are dated and you want to understand them: what was 'Watergate fatigue' really like in '74, what does 'jamming Marshall stacks' mean. The activity is intrinsically slow. You read a paragraph, you sit with it, you ask one quiet question. There's no rush. You want the AI to share the register — careful, present, willing to sit in the texture of it.
beats: Quote a short passage from a letter, ask one focused question
Reflect briefly on what it tells you about your father, then move to the next letter
Quiet pacing throughout — don't pile on questions, let things land

scenarios.name: Quietly thinking through a regret about an old friendship
scenarios.situation: It's 11pm and you're sitting at your kitchen table with a glass of wine, thinking about a friendship that ended badly six years ago. You haven't thought about it in months but tonight a song came on that reminded you of her. You're not in crisis — you're just turning it over. You opened the AI because sometimes thinking out loud helps you see something you couldn't see alone. You want a thinking partner, not a therapist or a problem-solver. The conversation should feel like talking to a wise older friend over a slow drink — measured, honest, comfortable with silences.
beats: Lay out the situation slowly — what happened, what you're stuck on
Push back gently if the AI moves to advice mode; you want to think, not be fixed
Stay in the contemplative register; don't escalate emotion or pace

scenarios.name: Translating a haiku word-by-word from Japanese
scenarios.situation: You're an amateur student of Japanese and you've found an old haiku you want to translate carefully into English. The poem is sixteen syllables long. You know the literal meaning of each word but you want the AI's help making the translation breathe — does this kanji carry seasonal weight? What does this particle do to the rhythm? The activity is intrinsically slow and word-by-word. You want the AI to match the patience: examine each phrase carefully, suggest two or three alternatives, let you sit with each before moving on. No bullet points, no markdown, no rushed lists. The poem deserves slow attention.
beats: Quote one line, ask about a single word or particle
Try out a phrasing, ask the AI which alternates feel closer
Move on to the next line only when the previous one feels right

scenarios.name: Watching dawn break, asking about how light bends
scenarios.situation: You're standing at your kitchen window watching dawn break over the hills. You've been up since 4am — couldn't sleep — and you've just noticed how the very first light isn't a wash of brightness but specific colours appearing in a specific order: deep blue, then orange in a thin band at the horizon, then a kind of grey-pink between them. You're curious why. You opened the AI to ask. It's quiet in the house, you have your tea, you're not in any hurry. You want the AI to explain like someone who is also looking out at a dawn — patient, observant, comfortable with ideas that don't fully resolve. Big enthusiastic explanations would jar against the moment.
beats: Describe what you're seeing, ask the focused question
Take in the answer slowly, ask one or two follow-ups
Let the conversation taper as the sun fully rises — no need to keep it going

scenarios.name: Drafting a letter to an estranged sibling, choosing words carefully
scenarios.situation: You haven't spoken to your older sister in four years. You've decided to write her a letter — actual paper, not email — and you're trying to get the first paragraph right. You don't want to be performatively warm or falsely apologetic; you also don't want to be cold. Every word matters because she'll read this once and decide whether to read the rest. You're using the AI to think through phrasings. The activity demands quiet care: you propose a sentence, you and the AI sit with what it implies, you try a variant. The AI being effusive or rushing to suggest five options would be exactly wrong. You want one suggestion at a time, considered.
beats: Share a draft sentence and what's bothering you about it
Discuss what specific words connote — what 'I've been thinking about you' actually says
Settle each phrasing carefully before moving on; no rapid-fire alternatives
